title: Observation antibunching with classical light in a linear interferometer
authors: Yu Gu, Yuhan Ma, Yiqi Song, Meixue Chen, Hui Chen, Huaibin Zheng, Yuchen He, Yu Zhou, Fuli Li, Zhuo Xu, Jianbin Liu
abstract: Understanding the boundary between classical and nonclassical phenomena is important for both fundamental researches in quantum optics and applications in quantum information. One of the most interesting research directions in this field is exploring nonclassical effects with classical light. In this paper, we will show that it is possible to observe antibunching with thermal light in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer by treating single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving detectors to perform photon-number projection measurements. Both temporal and spatial antibunching is observed via the correlation of two detectors detecting one and zero photon, respectively. By comparing the measured results of thermal and laser light, it is found that the observed antibunching arises from the combined effect of photon statistics of thermal light and photon-number projection measurement.The classical and nonclassical nature of the observed antibunching is analyzed. The results are helpful to understand the connection between classical and nonclassical correlation and may find applications in multiphoton interference and quantum imaging.
summary: This paper demonstrates that photon antibunching can be observed using classical thermal light by utilizing detectors as photon-number-resolving tools. The effect arises from the interplay between thermal photon statistics and photon-number projection measurements. This finding helps clarify the connection between classical and nonclassical correlations in optical systems.
main_problem: Investigating the boundary between classical and nonclassical phenomena by exploring whether photon antibunching, typically a nonclassical effect, can be observed using classical thermal light.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that antibunching can be observed with thermal light in a modified HBT interferometer through photon-number projection measurements, specifically via the $g^{(2)}_{10}$ correlation function.
method: The study combines a theoretical framework using a joint probability generating function with an experimental setup using a modified Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer and single-photon detectors acting as photon-number-resolving detectors.
model_or_system: A modified Hanbury Brown-Twiss (HBT) interferometer using a thermal light source (generated via a laser and rotating ground glass) and a 1:1 non-polarizing beam splitter, with single-photon detectors used for photon-number projection.
key_observables: Normalized second-order correlation functions $g^{(2)}_{mn}$, specifically $g^{(2)}_{11}$ and $g^{(2)}_{10}$, and the degree of first-order coherence $\mu$.
important_parameters: Average photon number $\bar{n}$, degree of coherence $\mu$, and the transition regime from antibunching to bunching as $\bar{n}$ increases.
main_assumptions: The detectors are treated as photon-number-resolving detectors via photon-number projection measurements.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of the modified HBT interferometer; Figures 8(a) and 8(b) illustrate the transition of the $g^{(2)}_{10}$ correlation from antibunching to bunching as the average photon number increases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of classical vs nonclassical boundaries, describes the experimental setup and theoretical model, presents results for various correlation functions and photon numbers, and concludes with an analysis of the nature of the observed effect.
why_interesting: It challenges the traditional association of antibunching with nonclassical light and provides insight into how measurement projection can induce nonclassical-like signatures in classical statistical distributions, which is relevant for quantum imaging and multiphoton interference.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Scheme for the modified HBT interferometer. Li: Thermal (i=T) or laser (i=L) light. BS: Non-polarizing 1 : 1 beam splitter. D1 and D2: Single-photon detectors. PTR: Photon detection time series recording system.

FIG. 2. The dependence of g(2) 11 (0) on ¯n and µ. g(2) 11 (0) is the normalized second-order correlation function when the two single-photon detection events at D1 and D2 are at the same space-time coordinate. ¯n is the average number of detected photons and µ is the squared modulus of the first-order co- herence function of thermal light.
FIG. 3. The dependence of g(2) 10 (0) on ¯n and µ. The meanings of the symbols are similar as the ones in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Experimental setup to observe antibunching with classical light. Laser: Single-mode continuous-wave laser. VA: Variable light intensity attenuator. L: Lens. RG: Rotat- ing groundglass. FC1 and FC2: Fiber couplers. HydraHarp 400: Photon detection time series recording system, Hydra- Harp 400, which can be replaced by other module with similar functions.

FIG. 4. The dependence of g(2) mn(0) on ¯n and µ. (a1) and (a2) show the relation between g(2) mn(0) and µ for ¯n equaling 1 and 5, respectively. (b1) and (b2) show the relation be- tween g(2) mn(0) and ¯n for µ equaling 0.5 and 1, respectively. g(2)(0) = 1+µ is the degree of second-order coherence of par- tially coherent thermal light and is drawn in these figures for comparison.
